messages.content: You are a CNC G-code assistant specializing in Fanuc-style milling programs. Explain code clearly, identify safety issues, and help interpret machining data.

Explain this G-code block line by line:
G21
G90
G17
G54
T01 M06
M03 S1200
G00 X0 Y0
G00 Z5.0
G01 Z-2.0 F100
G01 X50.0 F200
M05
G00 Z50.0
M30

Here is a line-by-line breakdown of this program:

G21 — Sets the control to metric (millimeter) input mode. All coordinates will be interpreted in mm.
G90 — Activates absolute positioning mode. All X, Y, Z values are measured from the work coordinate origin.
G17 — Selects the XY plane for circular interpolation and cutter compensation. This is the standard plane for vertical milling.
G54 — Activates work coordinate offset #1. The machine will reference all moves from the zero point defined under G54 in the offset table.
T01 M06 — Selects tool number 1 from the carousel and executes a tool change (M06). The machine will halt and swap to T01.
M03 S1200 — Starts the spindle in the clockwise direction at 1200 RPM.
G00 X0 Y0 — Rapid-travels the tool to X=0, Y=0 in the XY plane. No cutting occurs; this is a positioning move.
G00 Z5.0 — Rapid-moves the tool down to Z=5.0 mm above the workpiece surface, establishing a safe approach height.
G01 Z-2.0 F100 — Feeds the tool into the material at Z=-2.0 mm (2 mm depth of cut) at a controlled feed rate of 100 mm/min.
G01 X50.0 F200 — Performs a linear cutting move to X=50.0 mm at 200 mm/min. The tool cuts a horizontal slot while at depth.
M05 — Stops the spindle.
G00 Z50.0 — Rapid-retracts the tool to Z=50.0 mm, a safe clearance height above the workpiece and any fixtures.
M30 — Ends the program and rewinds it to the beginning. The machine resets and is ready for the next cycle.
You are a CNC G-code assistant specializing in Fanuc-style milling programs. Explain code clearly, identify safety issues, and help interpret machining data.
What does G00 do differently from G01?

G00 and G01 both move the tool, but they behave very differently:

G00 — Rapid Positioning. The machine moves each axis at its maximum programmed rapid rate (set by the machine builder, typically 10,000–30,000 mm/min). The axes may move simultaneously but not necessarily in a coordinated straight line, meaning the actual path can be diagonal or stairstepped depending on the control. G00 is used for non-cutting moves: repositioning above the part, retracting to a clearance height, or moving between features. No feed rate (F word) is needed or applied.

G01 — Linear Interpolation. The machine moves the tool in a perfectly straight line at the exact feed rate specified by the F word (e.g., F200 for 200 mm/min). All axes involved are coordinated so the tool travels on a precise linear path. G01 is used for all cutting moves — facing, profiling, slotting, plunging into material.

Key safety rule: Never use G00 to move into or through material. Because G00 does not guarantee a straight-line path and moves at maximum speed, it can crash the tool or cause severe damage. Always use G01 with an appropriate feed rate for any move where the tool contacts the workpiece.
You are a CNC G-code assistant specializing in Fanuc-style milling programs. Explain code clearly, identify safety issues, and help interpret machining data.

What is wrong with this G-code?
G21
T02 M06
G01 X50 Y50 F200
M03 S800
M30

This program has several serious issues:

1. Spindle started after cutting move: M03 S800 appears after the G01 cutting move. This means the program attempts to feed the tool through the material while the spindle is not rotating. Dragging a stationary tool through material will break the tool, damage the workpiece, and potentially cause a machine crash.

2. No work coordinate offset: There is no G54 (or other G5x offset) specified. The machine will reference machine zero (G53), which is almost never the intended cutting location and could result in the tool moving to an unexpected or dangerous position.

3. No safe Z clearance move: The program goes directly to a G01 XY move without first establishing a safe Z height. If the tool is near the part surface, this lateral move could drag the tool across the workpiece.

4. No modal setup block: There is no G90 (absolute mode), G17 (plane selection), or explicit positioning mode declared. The machine inherits whatever modal state was left from the previous program.

Here is a corrected version:
G21 G90 G17
G54
T02 M06
M03 S800
G00 Z50.0
G00 X50.0 Y50.0
G01 Z-2.0 F100
G01 X100.0 F200
M05
G00 Z50.0
M30

This version starts the spindle before any movement, establishes the work offset, moves to a safe Z height first, and stops the spindle before retracting.
You are a CNC G-code assistant specializing in Fanuc-style milling programs. Explain code clearly, identify safety issues, and help interpret machining data.
Explain what M03, M04, and M05 do.

These are spindle control miscellaneous functions (M codes):

M03 — Spindle On, Clockwise (CW). Starts the spindle rotating in the forward (clockwise when viewed from above the spindle nose) direction at the speed set by the S word (e.g., M03 S1200 starts at 1200 RPM). This is the correct direction for standard right-hand cutting tools in milling operations.

M04 — Spindle On, Counter-Clockwise (CCW). Starts the spindle in the reverse direction. M04 is used for left-hand tools, certain tapping cycles (depending on the control), and specialized operations. Using M04 with a standard right-hand end mill will cause the tool to rub rather than cut and will likely damage the tool and workpiece.

M05 — Spindle Stop. Commands the spindle to decelerate and stop. It should be used before a tool change, before measuring, before manually inspecting the part, or before program end. On most Fanuc controls, M06 (tool change) will automatically stop the spindle, but it is good practice to program M05 explicitly before M06 for clarity and safety.

Important: Always verify the S word (spindle speed) is appropriate for the tool diameter and material before commanding M03. Overspeeding a large diameter tool or face mill can be extremely dangerous.
You are a CNC G-code assistant specializing in Fanuc-style milling programs. Explain code clearly, identify safety issues, and help interpret machining data.
What does G54 mean, and why does it matter?

G54 is the command to activate Work Coordinate System (WCS) offset number 1. It tells the CNC control to shift its reference point from the machine's absolute home position (machine zero) to a user-defined zero point that corresponds to the part being machined.

Why it matters:

A CNC machine has a fixed machine zero (also called machine home or G53 origin), typically located at the extreme travel limit of each axis. This position is almost never where you want the tool to cut. Instead, operators probe and set a part zero — usually a corner, hole center, or datum surface on the workpiece — and store those offset distances in the G54 register.

When G54 is active, G00 X0 Y0 Z0 moves the tool to the part zero you set, not to machine zero. This means your G-code program can always be written as if X0 Y0 is the part origin, regardless of where on the machine table the part is fixtured.

Fanuc controls support up to six standard work offsets: G54 through G59. Extended offsets (G54.1 P1 through G54.1 P48 or more) are available on most modern controls.

If no work offset is specified, the machine defaults to G53 (machine coordinates), and the tool may rapid to an entirely wrong and potentially dangerous location. Always verify the correct G54–G59 offset is active and contains the correct values before starting a program on a new setup.
You are a CNC G-code assistant specializing in Fanuc-style milling programs. Explain code clearly, identify safety issues, and help interpret machining data.

Find the problems in this G-code:
G21 G90 G17
G54
T01 M06
M03 S1200
G00 X10 Y10
G01 Z-5 F50
G01 X60 Y10
G01 X60 Y50
G01 X10 Y50
G01 X10 Y10
G00 Z50
M30

This program is mostly well-structured but has one notable issue and a few best-practice improvements:

Issue — No Z clearance move before the first XY rapid:
After the tool change (T01 M06), the spindle starts (M03 S1200) and then G00 rapids to X10 Y10. However, there is no G00 Z50.0 (or similar clearance height move) before the XY rapid. Depending on the Z position left from the previous operation or tool change, the tool could be sitting at a low Z position and could collide with the part or fixture during the XY rapid move.

Recommendation — Add a Z clearance move immediately after M03:
G00 Z50.0
G00 X10.0 Y10.0

Additional observations:
- The program closes the rectangular profile back to X10 Y10 but does not return to a safe Z height until after the final G01. This is correct since it is a closed contour, but verify the tool does not lift prematurely.
- M05 (spindle stop) is missing before M30. While many controls auto-stop the spindle at M30, explicitly programming M05 before program end is safer and clearer.
- Feed rate F50 for the plunge move is reasonable for a small tool but should be verified against the tool manufacturer's recommended plunge feed.

Corrected structure:
G21 G90 G17
G54
T01 M06
M03 S1200
G00 Z50.0
G00 X10.0 Y10.0
G01 Z-5.0 F50
G01 X60.0 Y10.0 F200
G01 X60.0 Y50.0
G01 X10.0 Y50.0
G01 X10.0 Y10.0
M05
G00 Z50.0
M30
You are a CNC G-code assistant specializing in Fanuc-style milling programs. Explain code clearly, identify safety issues, and help interpret machining data.
Explain G02 and G03 with an example.

G02 and G03 are circular interpolation commands. They move the tool along an arc at a controlled feed rate.

G02 — Clockwise arc (CW) in the currently active plane (G17 = XY plane).
G03 — Counter-clockwise arc (CCW) in the currently active plane.

To define an arc you need:
- The end point (X, Y coordinates of where the arc ends)
- The arc center offset from the start point using I (X-axis offset) and J (Y-axis offset), OR the radius using R (positive R = minor arc under 180°, negative R = major arc over 180°)
- A feed rate (F word)

Example — Cutting a clockwise quarter-circle with radius 20 mm:

G21 G90 G17
G54
T01 M06
M03 S1200
G00 Z50.0
G00 X0.0 Y20.0
G01 Z-3.0 F80
G02 X20.0 Y0.0 R20.0 F200
M05
G00 Z50.0
M30

Starting at X0 Y20, the arc sweeps clockwise to X20 Y0. The radius is 20 mm. The arc center is at X0 Y0 (the origin), which is 0 mm in X and -20 mm in J from the start point. Using I/J notation the same move would be:
G02 X20.0 Y0.0 I0.0 J-20.0 F200

Key rule: Always confirm CW vs CCW direction relative to the active machining plane (G17 for XY). Swapping G02 for G03 will cut the arc in the opposite direction, which changes the tool path and can cause gouging or a wrong profile.
You are a CNC G-code assistant specializing in Fanuc-style milling programs. Explain code clearly, identify safety issues, and help interpret machining data.
What is the difference between G90 and G91?

G90 and G91 control how the CNC control interprets coordinate values in the program.

G90 — Absolute Positioning. Every coordinate is measured from the active work coordinate origin (e.g., the G54 zero point). If you program G00 X50.0, the tool always moves to the location that is 50 mm from the work origin, regardless of where the tool currently is.

G91 — Incremental Positioning. Every coordinate is a distance and direction relative to the tool's current position. If the tool is at X20.0 and you program G00 X50.0 in G91 mode, the tool moves to X70.0 in absolute terms (current position + increment).

Example:
In G90: G01 X100.0 moves the tool to the absolute position X=100 mm from origin.
In G91: G01 X100.0 moves the tool 100 mm in the positive X direction from wherever it currently is.

Best practice: Write production programs primarily in G90 (absolute). Absolute programs are much easier to verify, edit, and debug because each line tells you exactly where the tool will be. G91 is useful for repetitive patterns, subroutines, and certain canned cycle parameters, but mixing G90 and G91 carelessly in a single program is a common source of positioning errors and crashes. Always clearly document any G91 sections and switch back to G90 when finished.
You are a CNC G-code assistant specializing in Fanuc-style milling programs. Explain code clearly, identify safety issues, and help interpret machining data.

What is wrong with this G-code?
G21 G90 G17
G54
T03 M06
M03 S2500
G00 Z50.0
G00 X0 Y0
G00 Z-10.0
G01 X100 F300
M05
G00 Z50.0
M30

This program has one critical safety error:

Critical Issue — Rapid move (G00) into the workpiece:
The line G00 Z-10.0 uses a rapid positioning command (G00) to plunge 10 mm below the work surface (Z=0 is typically the top face of the part). G00 moves at maximum machine rapid speed with no feed rate control. Plunging into material at rapid speed will instantly break the tool, damage the workpiece, and could cause a machine crash or injury.

Any move into the material must use G01 (or G02/G03 for arcs) with an appropriate plunge feed rate. A safe plunge feed rate is typically 30–50% of the horizontal cutting feed rate, depending on the tool and material.

Additional observation:
Z-10.0 is a direct plunge to full depth. For deeper cuts or harder materials, consider ramping into the material or using multiple depth passes to avoid overloading the tool.

Corrected version:
G21 G90 G17
G54
T03 M06
M03 S2500
G00 Z50.0
G00 X0.0 Y0.0
G01 Z-10.0 F120
G01 X100.0 F300
M05
G00 Z50.0
M30

The plunge is now a controlled G01 move at F120 mm/min, which is 40% of the horizontal feed rate, giving the tool a safe entry into the material.
You are a CNC G-code assistant specializing in Fanuc-style milling programs. Explain code clearly, identify safety issues, and help interpret machining data.
What does a canned drilling cycle G81 do? Show me an example.

G81 is a standard drilling canned cycle on Fanuc controls. It automates the repetitive sequence of: rapid to position → feed to depth → rapid retract. You define the cycle once and it executes at each XY position you specify afterward.

G81 syntax:
G81 X__ Y__ Z__ R__ F__

X, Y — Hole position in the XY plane.
Z — Final depth of the hole (must be negative if drilling below the work surface in G54).
R — R-plane (retract plane). The tool rapids from the initial Z height to the R-plane, then feeds from R to Z depth. R is usually set just above the part surface, e.g., R2.0.
F — Feed rate for the drilling pass.

Example — Drilling two holes to 15 mm depth:
G21 G90 G17
G54
T02 M06
M03 S1800
G00 Z50.0
G81 X20.0 Y20.0 Z-15.0 R2.0 F120
X60.0 Y20.0
G80
M05
G00 Z50.0
M30

Line-by-line:
G81 — Activates the drilling canned cycle. The tool rapids to X20 Y20, descends from Z50 to R2.0 at rapid, then feeds to Z-15.0 at F120, then rapids back to Z50.
X60.0 Y20.0 — The canned cycle repeats automatically at the new position (X60 Y20) because G81 is still active (modal).
G80 — Cancels the active canned cycle. Always program G80 after you are done drilling. Failing to cancel the cycle means any subsequent XY move will trigger another drilling operation.
You are a CNC G-code assistant specializing in Fanuc-style milling programs. Explain code clearly, identify safety issues, and help interpret machining data.
I'm seeing this error data from our machine: Cycle time increased from 4.2 min to 6.8 min over 3 weeks. No program changes. What could cause this?

A 62% increase in cycle time with no program changes is a significant warning sign. Here are the most likely causes, roughly in order of probability:

1. Reduced cutting speeds due to tool wear:
As tools wear, operators or the machine adaptive control may reduce feed rates and spindle speeds to compensate. Worn tools also require more passes or slower feeds to achieve acceptable surface finish. Check tool life counters and inspect cutting edges under magnification.

2. Increased dwell or tool change time:
If the ATC (automatic tool changer) is slow due to worn cams, low air pressure, or lubrication issues, each tool change adds seconds that accumulate across many cycles.

3. Servo or drive performance degradation:
Worn ball screws, loose gibs, or degraded servo drive parameters cause axes to accelerate and decelerate more slowly than programmed, stretching the actual time of every rapid and feed move.

4. Coolant or chip management interruptions:
If the operator is stopping the machine to clear chips or address coolant flow problems, those pauses extend cycle time without any alarm being logged.

5. Spindle speed not reaching setpoint:
A failing spindle motor or VFD (variable frequency drive) may take longer to reach commanded speed, adding dwell before feeds begin. Check the actual spindle speed feedback vs. commanded speed.

Recommended actions:
- Pull the machine's actual feed rate and spindle override settings from the operator panel logs.
- Inspect and replace worn tooling.
- Check air pressure, lubrication levels, and ATC function.
- Run a ballbar test or check axis positioning accuracy if servo issues are suspected.
You are a CNC G-code assistant specializing in Fanuc-style milling programs. Explain code clearly, identify safety issues, and help interpret machining data.
Explain what T01 M06 does and what happens physically on the machine.

T01 M06 is the standard Fanuc tool change command. Here is what each part means and what the machine does:

T01 — Tool Select. This tells the control which tool number to prepare. The ATC (Automatic Tool Changer) carousel or turret rotates to index tool pocket 1 into the ready position. On many machines, T and M06 can be on the same line; on others, T is pre-called one line earlier so the carousel indexes during the previous operation, saving time.

M06 — Tool Change Execute. This triggers the actual physical exchange:
1. The machine moves to the tool change position (a safe fixed point defined by the machine builder, usually high Z and a specific XY).
2. The spindle stops if it has not already been stopped by M05.
3. The spindle orientates to a fixed angular position (spindle orientation, M19 is sometimes called explicitly) so the tool can be properly gripped.
4. The ATC arm swings in, grips both the current spindle tool and the waiting T01 tool, extracts both, rotates 180°, and inserts the new tool into the spindle.
5. The old tool goes back into the carousel pocket.
6. The ATC arm retracts.
7. The control signals ready, and the program continues to the next line.

Important: After M06, the spindle is stopped and at the tool change Z position. You must program M03 (spindle on) and G00 to your next position before any cutting moves. Never assume the spindle is running or the tool is in position after a tool change.

What is wrong with this program?
G21 G90 G17
G54
T01 M06
M03 S1200
G00 Z50.0
G00 X0 Y0
G01 Z-3.0 F80
G02 X30.0 Y0.0 I15.0 J0.0 F200
G00 Z50.0
M30

The program is mostly correct but has one geometric error and a missing best-practice step:

Geometric Issue — Incorrect I/J values for the arc:
The arc starts at X0 Y0 and is supposed to end at X30 Y0 with a G02 (clockwise) move. The I15.0 J0.0 specifies that the arc center is 15 mm in the positive X direction from the start point, placing the center at X15 Y0. This gives a radius of 15 mm. However, the end point X30 Y0 is also exactly 15 mm from the center X15 Y0, so the geometry is actually valid — this is a valid semicircle of radius 15 mm sweeping clockwise from X0 Y0 to X30 Y0.

If the intent was a full 360° circle, the end point must equal the start point (X0 Y0), not X30 Y0. Verify the intended shape.

Missing — M05 before M30:
The spindle is not stopped before the program ends. While M30 will stop the spindle on most Fanuc controls, explicitly programming M05 before G00 Z50.0 and M30 is safer and more portable across different machine models.

Best-practice addition:
....
M05
G00 Z50.0
M30

Everything else — modal setup, work offset, tool change sequence, Z clearance before XY rapids, and controlled plunge — is correctly structured.
You are a CNC G-code assistant specializing in Fanuc-style milling programs. Explain code clearly, identify safety issues, and help interpret machining data.
What does G43 H01 do?

G43 H01 activates Tool Length Compensation (TLC), also called Tool Length Offset, using the value stored in offset register H01.

G43 — Apply tool length compensation in the positive direction. This tells the control to add the value from the H register to all Z-axis movements. In practice, this compensates for the actual measured length of the tool in the spindle so that Z0 in the program corresponds to the correct position on the workpiece, regardless of how long the tool physically is.

H01 — References offset register number 1. The tool length value (measured at the tool setter or presetter) is stored here by the operator or tool setter before the program runs. For example, if H01 contains 125.347, every Z move is offset by that amount.

Why it matters:
Without G43, every tool would need the program's Z coordinates rewritten to account for its specific physical length. With G43, you measure each tool, store the result in its H register, and the control automatically adjusts Z positions so the program works correctly regardless of tool length variation.

Typical usage:
T01 M06
G43 H01 Z50.0
M03 S1200

Here G43 H01 is activated on the same line as the first Z move, which is the recommended practice on Fanuc controls. G49 cancels tool length compensation. Always cancel G43 with G49 or by calling G28 Z0 before a tool change to avoid compounding offset errors between tools.
You are a CNC G-code assistant specializing in Fanuc-style milling programs. Explain code clearly, identify safety issues, and help interpret machining data.
Our tool wear data shows: End mill #3 average life = 45 minutes. Last 5 runs: 38, 41, 43, 44, 40 minutes. Is this normal variation or a problem?

This data shows a healthy, consistent tool life pattern. Here is the analysis:

Statistical summary:
- Average of last 5 runs: (38+41+43+44+40) / 5 = 41.2 minutes
- Range: 38–44 minutes (a spread of 6 minutes)
- Variation from expected average (45 min): approximately 8.4% below average

Interpretation:
The variation between runs (6-minute spread) is about 13% of the mean, which is acceptable for a milling operation. Tool life is inherently variable due to small differences in material hardness, coolant delivery consistency, chip evacuation, and minor insert-to-insert variation within a grade.

The consistent drift slightly below the 45-minute baseline (41.2 vs 45.0) could indicate:
- The 45-minute target was set under more favorable conditions (fresher coolant, new machine, softer material lot)
- Gradual process drift — worth monitoring over the next 10–15 runs
- Coolant concentration slightly lower than optimal, reducing lubrication

Recommendations:
- No immediate action required. Continue monitoring.
- Check coolant concentration (refractometer reading) — it should be within the recommended range for your material.
- If the average drops below 35 minutes (22% below target) or you see a sudden single run below 30 minutes, investigate cutting parameters, coolant delivery, and material certification.
- Consider updating the baseline average to 41 minutes to reflect current conditions and avoid premature or late tool changes.
You are a CNC G-code assistant specializing in Fanuc-style milling programs. Explain code clearly, identify safety issues, and help interpret machining data.
What does M08 and M09 do in a CNC program?

M08 and M09 control the flood coolant system:

M08 — Coolant On. Activates the flood coolant pump, which delivers cutting fluid to the tool-workpiece interface. Coolant performs several critical functions: it reduces heat at the cutting zone (extending tool life), lubricates the cut (reducing friction and built-up edge on the tool), and flushes chips away from the cutting area (preventing re-cutting of chips, which degrades surface finish and damages tools).

M09 — Coolant Off. Shuts down the coolant pump. M09 is typically programmed before a tool change, before program end, and during any inspection pauses.

Typical placement in a program:
T01 M06
M03 S1200
M08
G00 Z50.0
G00 X0 Y0
G01 Z-3.0 F80
... (cutting moves) ...
M09
M05
G00 Z50.0
M30

Additional coolant M codes available on many Fanuc machines:
- M07 — Mist coolant on (air/coolant mist, used for aluminum or where flood would cause thermal shock)
- M50 / M51 — Through-spindle coolant on/off (TSC), where coolant is delivered directly through the tool for deep hole drilling or high-pressure applications

Best practice: Always turn coolant off (M09) before retracting to the tool change position. Coolant spraying during a tool change can contaminate the ATC mechanism and create slip hazards.

What is wrong with this G-code?
G20
G90 G17
G54
T01 M06
M03 S1200
G00 Z2.0
G00 X0 Y0
G01 Z-0.125 F5.0
G01 X4.0 F10.0
M05
G00 Z2.0
M30

This program appears to be written in inch mode (G20), which changes how you read the values. Let me evaluate it in that context:

G20 — Inch mode. All coordinates and feed rates are in inches. This is valid and consistent throughout the program.

Observation — Feed rate F5.0 for the plunge:
In inch mode, F5.0 means 5.0 inches per minute. This is a reasonable plunge feed for a small end mill in steel or aluminum. However, if someone reads this as millimeters per minute, 5.0 mm/min is extremely slow and suggests a misunderstanding. Always document units clearly.

Observation — F10.0 for horizontal cutting:
10.0 inches per minute. This is moderate and likely appropriate depending on tool diameter and material.

Missing — Tool Length Compensation (G43):
There is no G43 H__ command. Without tool length compensation, Z0 in the program may not correspond to the top of the workpiece unless the tool was set using the work offset Z value directly (touching off the tool to the part surface and storing in G54 Z). If TLC is expected on this machine's setup, omitting G43 will cause the Z depth to be wrong.

Missing — M05 before retract is correct, but M09 is absent if coolant was used in earlier operations — verify coolant state.

If the machine uses G43/G49 for tool length management, add:
G43 H01 Z2.0
immediately after M03 and before the first Z rapid. Otherwise the program logic is sound.
You are a CNC G-code assistant specializing in Fanuc-style milling programs. Explain code clearly, identify safety issues, and help interpret machining data.
Explain what G28 does and when to use it.

G28 commands the machine to return to its machine home (reference) position — the absolute mechanical zero point established by the machine builder during installation and homing.

Syntax: G28 X0 Y0 Z0 (or any combination of axes)

How it works:
G28 causes the specified axes to first move to an intermediate point defined by the X Y Z values in the G28 block, and then continue home to the machine reference position. This intermediate point is a safety measure — it allows you to clear fixtures or the part before the axis rapids home.

For example:
G28 Z0
This first moves Z to the intermediate point Z=0 in the current coordinate system (the top of the part in G54), then rapids Z to machine zero (the top of Z travel). This ensures the tool clears the workpiece before the Y or X axes move home.

Common uses:
1. Before a tool change when not using a fixed tool change position: G28 Z0 retracts Z safely first.
2. At program end to park the machine in a known position.
3. After a crash or alarm to re-establish machine position.
4. As part of a reference return after power-up if the machine does not auto-home.

Important cautions:
- G28 moves at rapid speed. Make sure the path from current position to the intermediate point and then to home is clear of clamps, fixtures, and the part.
- Always specify Z first in a G28 sequence: G28 Z0 before G28 X0 Y0. This lifts the tool before moving in XY, preventing the tool from dragging across the part.
You are a CNC G-code assistant specializing in Fanuc-style milling programs. Explain code clearly, identify safety issues, and help interpret machining data.
Our spindle load data shows: Normal = 40–55%. Last 10 cycles averaged 78%. No program changes. What does this mean?

A spindle load averaging 78% against a normal baseline of 40–55% represents an overload condition of roughly 40–90% above expected. This is a serious indicator that requires immediate investigation before continuing production.

Most likely causes:

1. Severe tool wear or breakage:
A worn or chipped cutting edge requires significantly more force to push through material. This is the most common cause of sudden load spikes. Inspect the tool immediately — even if it has not triggered a tool breakage sensor, the edge may be severely worn.

2. Wrong tool in the spindle:
Verify that the correct tool number, diameter, and grade is loaded. A larger-diameter tool or wrong geometry will draw more power than expected.

3. Incorrect spindle speed or feed rate:
If an operator manually adjusted overrides (spindle speed override or feed rate override), the machine may be running feeds and speeds outside the programmed values. Check that overrides are at 100%.

4. Material hardness variation:
A harder material lot, hardened skin layer (castings), or inclusion in the stock will increase cutting resistance.

5. Coolant failure:
If coolant flow has decreased or stopped, cutting temperatures rise, increasing friction and effective tool load.

Immediate actions:
- Stop production and inspect the tool.
- Check operator override settings.
- Verify coolant flow and concentration.
- Review spindle speed and feed rate against the process sheet.
- If the tool is worn, replace it, run one part, and verify load returns to 40–55% before resuming full production.

What is G41 and G42 cutter compensation? Show a simple example.

G41 and G42 are cutter radius compensation (CRC) commands. They offset the programmed tool path by the radius of the cutting tool so you can program the part geometry directly (the nominal edge of the part) rather than calculating offset paths for each tool diameter.

G41 — Cutter compensation left. The tool is offset to the left of the programmed direction of travel. Used for conventional or climb milling on the outside of a profile when moving counter-clockwise.
G42 — Cutter compensation right. The tool is offset to the right of the programmed direction of travel. Typically used for outside profiles milled clockwise.

D word — Specifies which offset register holds the cutter radius. D01 might contain 5.0 (for a 10 mm diameter tool with 5 mm radius).

Simple outside profile example (programming the part edge, letting the control offset the path):

G21 G90 G17
G54
T01 M06
M03 S1200
G00 Z50.0
G00 X-10.0 Y-10.0
G01 Z-3.0 F80
G42 D01 G01 X0.0 Y0.0 F200
G01 X50.0 Y0.0
G01 X50.0 Y30.0
G01 X0.0 Y30.0
G01 X0.0 Y0.0
G40 G01 X-10.0 Y-10.0
M05
G00 Z50.0
M30

G42 is activated on a lead-in move (approaching from outside the part). G40 cancels compensation on the lead-out move, also outside the part. Critical rule: Never activate (G41/G42) or cancel (G40) cutter compensation directly on the part edge — always on a lead-in or lead-out move to prevent gouging or incorrect geometry.

Critical Issue — G91 (Incremental mode) used when G90 (Absolute) is likely intended:
The program opens with G21 G91 G17. G91 means all coordinates are incremental (relative to current position). This affects every subsequent move:
- G00 X25.0 Y25.0 moves 25 mm from wherever the machine currently is — not to a fixed location.
- G01 X50.0 moves 50 mm in X from the current position.
- G01 X-50.0 moves 50 mm back in the negative X direction.

In G91, the start position is entirely dependent on where the previous program left the machine, making this program unpredictable and dangerous in a production context.

What is the purpose of the S word in a CNC program?

The S word sets the spindle speed. It is followed by a number representing the commanded rotational speed in RPM (revolutions per minute) when used with G97, or surface feet per minute / surface meters per minute when used with G96 (constant surface speed mode, more common on lathes).

In standard Fanuc milling:
S1200 — Commands the spindle to rotate at 1200 RPM.

The S word alone does not start the spindle. It only sets the target speed. The spindle is started by M03 (clockwise) or M04 (counter-clockwise). These can be combined on the same line:
M03 S1200

How to calculate correct spindle speed:
For milling, use the surface feet per minute (SFM) or surface meters per minute (SMM) recommended by the tool manufacturer for the material:

RPM = (SFM × 3.82) / Tool Diameter (inches)
or
RPM = (SMM × 1000) / (π × Tool Diameter in mm)

Example: A 10 mm end mill in aluminum at 200 SMM:
RPM = (200 × 1000) / (3.14159 × 10) = 6,366 RPM

Key considerations:
- Never exceed the maximum RPM rating of the tool, holder, or machine spindle.
- For indexable cutters (face mills, insert end mills), always check the insert manufacturer's maximum speed — exceeding it can cause inserts to eject.
- The S value should be programmed before or on the same line as M03, never after, to ensure the spindle reaches the correct speed before cutting begins.

Explain what M30 does versus M02 and M00.

These three M codes all stop program execution but behave differently:

M00 — Program Stop (unconditional). Stops all machine motion, the spindle, and coolant. The operator must press Cycle Start to resume. M00 is used mid-program for mandatory pauses: part inspection, flipping a workpiece, manual clamping, or inserting a measurement step. The control retains the modal state (G90, feed rate, tool number, etc.) and resumes from the next line when Cycle Start is pressed.

M01 — Optional Stop. Identical to M00, but only activates if the Optional Stop switch on the operator panel is turned on. When the switch is off, M01 is ignored and the program runs through it. This allows operators to quickly disable mid-program pauses during production runs while keeping them available for setup and first-article inspection.

M02 — End of Program. Signals the end of the main program. The machine stops all motion, spindle, and coolant. On some Fanuc controls M02 rewinds the program cursor to the start; on others it does not. Behavior can vary between machine builders, which makes M02 less portable.

M30 — End of Program with Rewind. Stops all motion, spindle, and coolant, and unconditionally rewinds the program cursor to the beginning (the % or O-number line). M30 is the preferred program ending command on Fanuc controls because its behavior is consistent across machines. After M30, pressing Cycle Start immediately begins the next cycle without any manual rewind step.

Best practice: End all production programs with M05, then M09, then G00 Z50.0, then M30 — in that order — to ensure a clean, safe program end.

Our surface finish data shows Ra values increasing from 0.8 µm to 2.4 µm over a production run of 200 parts. The program has not changed. What is happening?

An Ra increase of 200% (0.8 to 2.4 µm) over a 200-part run without program changes is a classic progressive tool wear signature. Here is a systematic explanation:

Root cause — Gradual tool wear:
As the cutting edge wears, it transitions from a sharp, well-defined geometry to a rounded or chipped edge. A worn edge does not cleanly shear the material — it smears, plows, and tears it instead. This produces a rougher surface with higher peaks and valleys, directly increasing Ra.

Why it happens progressively:
Tool wear follows a predictable three-phase curve: an initial break-in phase (first 5–10% of tool life), a steady-state wear phase (low Ra, predictable performance), and an accelerating wear phase (rapid Ra degradation, your current situation). If the Ra started rising after ~150 parts, the tool likely entered the accelerating wear phase around that point.

Other contributing factors to rule out:
- Coolant concentration drift: check refractometer reading; low concentration increases friction and heat, degrading finish.
- Chip re-cutting: chips returning to the cut zone act as abrasive particles. Check coolant flow direction and volume.
- Spindle runout: if a bearing is degrading, radial runout increases, causing the tool to trace an inconsistent path. Measure runout with a test indicator.
- Vibration/chatter onset: check fixture clamping force and part consistency.

Actions:
- Replace or index the tool immediately.
- Establish a tool change interval (e.g., every 80 parts) to prevent this from recurring.
- Set an Ra control chart with a warning limit at 1.2 µm and an action limit at 1.6 µm so degradation is caught early.

What does G96 S200 mean and when would you use it in milling?

G96 activates Constant Surface Speed (CSS) mode, also called Constant Surface Footage (CSF) mode. When G96 S200 is programmed, the control automatically adjusts the spindle RPM as the tool diameter (or in turning, the workpiece diameter) changes to maintain a constant 200 surface meters per minute (SMM) — or 200 SFM if in G20 inch mode.

How it works:
The control continuously recalculates RPM = (S × 1000) / (π × diameter) as the effective cutting diameter changes, adjusting the spindle speed in real time.

In milling, G96 is most commonly used with:
1. Face milling with large diameter cutters — as the cutter passes over varying features (bosses, pockets), the effective cutting diameter can change.
2. Contour milling with varying radii — though this is more niche in milling than turning.
3. Trochoidal milling paths where the tool engagement arc varies.

Important safety requirement — G50 (maximum spindle speed clamp):
When using G96, always program a maximum RPM limit before activating CSS. If the calculated diameter approaches zero, the control will command extremely high RPM, which can dangerously overspeed the spindle:
G50 S6000 (limits spindle to max 6000 RPM)
G96 S200 M03

To return to standard RPM mode, program G97 followed by the desired RPM:
G97 S1200 M03

G96 is far more commonly used in CNC turning than milling. Most milling programs use G97 (direct RPM) exclusively because tool diameter is constant throughout a given cut.

Verify Q5.0 is appropriate for the drill diameter — for small drills (under 3 mm), smaller Q values (1.0–2.0 mm) are safer.

Our machine logged 47 alarm code 417 (servo alarm, X axis) events this month, up from 3 last month. What does this suggest?

A 15-fold increase in Fanuc Alarm 417 (Servo Alarm: X-axis — typically a following error or position error overrun) in one month is a critical maintenance signal. This alarm fires when the actual X-axis position deviates from the commanded position by more than the allowable threshold stored in parameter 1828.

What it means physically:
The servo system is struggling to keep the X axis where the control commands it to be. The motor is either unable to move the axis fast enough, or something is resisting motion and the position error accumulates beyond the limit.

Most likely causes (in order of probability):

1. Ball screw wear or damage: Increased friction in the X-axis ball screw from worn ball nuts, loss of lubrication, or contamination causes position lag. This is the most common cause of progressive alarm escalation.

2. Linear guide wear or binding: Worn or contaminated linear guide rails increase resistance to axis motion, overloading the servo.

3. Lubrication system failure: If the automatic lubrication system has a clogged line or empty reservoir, the ball screws and guides run dry. Check the lube unit immediately.

4. Servo motor or drive degradation: A failing encoder, worn motor brushes (on older DC servos), or a degrading servo amplifier can cause following error under load.

5. Loose axis coupling: A loose or damaged coupling between the servo motor and ball screw introduces mechanical slip.

Immediate actions:
- Check the automatic lubrication system oil level and cycle it manually.
- Inspect the X-axis ball screw for unusual noise or roughness by jogging slowly.
- Review the alarm timestamps — do they correlate with specific operations, feed rates, or times of day (suggesting thermal effects)?
- Contact your machine tool service engineer for a ballbar test and servo parameter review.

Explain what a safe startup block is and write one for a Fanuc milling program.

A safe startup block (also called a safe block or modal reset block) is a single G-code line placed at the beginning of a program to explicitly set all critical modal states to known, safe values. Its purpose is to override any unexpected modal conditions inherited from a previous program, a manual jog operation, or a control restart.

Without a safe startup block, your program inherits whatever modal state the machine was left in. For example, if a previous program used G91 (incremental mode) and did not cancel it, your new program's G00 moves will be incremental — often causing a crash.

A comprehensive Fanuc milling safe startup block:
G21 G90 G17 G40 G49 G80

Each code explained:
G21 — Metric mode (or G20 for inch). Explicitly sets units so no ambiguity.
G90 — Absolute positioning. Ensures all coordinates are referenced from the work origin.
G17 — XY plane select. Required for correct circular interpolation and cutter comp.
G40 — Cancel cutter radius compensation (G41/G42). Prevents unexpected tool offsets from a previous program.
G49 — Cancel tool length compensation. Clears any active G43/G44 offset.
G80 — Cancel canned cycles. Ensures no G81/G83/G84 cycle is still active.

Full program opening example:
O2001
G21 G90 G17 G40 G49 G80
G54
T01 M06
M03 S1200
G43 H01 Z50.0
M08
G00 X0.0 Y0.0
...

This block takes less than one second to execute and can prevent costly crashes and scrapped parts. It is considered mandatory best practice in any professional CNC programming environment.

What does G84 do in a Fanuc program?

G84 is the tapping canned cycle on Fanuc controls. It automates the sequence required for rigid tapping or floating-head tapping: feed to depth at a synchronized rate, reverse the spindle, and retract.

Syntax:
G84 X__ Y__ Z__ R__ F__

X, Y — Position of the hole to tap.
Z — Final depth of the threaded hole.
R — R-plane (retract plane, just above the hole entrance).
F — Feed rate. For tapping, feed rate must equal: pitch × RPM. For example, an M8×1.25 thread tapped at S600: F = 1.25 × 600 = 750 mm/min.

Rigid tapping (G84 with M29):
Modern Fanuc controls support rigid tapping, where the spindle is servo-controlled and synchronized precisely with the Z-axis feed. M29 S__ activates rigid tapping mode before G84. This eliminates the need for a floating tap holder and produces more accurate threads at higher speeds.

Example:
G21 G90 G17 G40 G49 G80
G54
T03 M06
G43 H03 Z50.0
M29 S600
M08
G84 X30.0 Y30.0 Z-18.0 R3.0 F750
X70.0
G80
M09
M05
G00 Z50.0
M30

Critical notes:
- Feed rate must be exact for the thread pitch and spindle speed. Even a small error will strip the thread or break the tap.
- G84 automatically reverses the spindle (M04) on the retract stroke and returns to M03 when clear of the hole.
- Always use G80 to cancel the cycle after all holes are complete.

This program uses cutter radius compensation (G41) and has one critical error:

Critical Issue — G41 activated at full cutting depth:
The cutter compensation G41 D01 is activated on a move at Z-5.0 (the tool is already 5 mm into the material). Cutter compensation must be activated during a lead-in move that is in air — not while the tool is engaged in the material. Activating G41 inside the cut causes the control to apply a sudden offset to the tool path, which will create a gouge, an incorrect feature, or a crash.

Correct sequence:
1. Rapid to the lead-in approach position in air (above the part).
2. Activate G41 on the lead-in move in the XY plane while at a safe Z height.
3. Then plunge to cutting depth.
4. Complete the profile.
5. Retract the tool.
6. Cancel G40 on the lead-out move, also in air.

Corrected approach:
G00 X-10.0 Y0.0 (approach in air)
G41 D01 G01 X0.0 Y0.0 F250 (activate CRC on lead-in, in air)
G01 Z-5.0 F100 (plunge now that CRC is active)
G01 X10.0 Y0.0
... (profile moves)
G01 Z5.0 F100 (retract before canceling)
G40 G01 X-10.0 Y0.0 (cancel CRC on lead-out, in air)

Always verify: the lead-in and lead-out moves must be longer than the cutter radius stored in D01, or the control will alarm on an illegal CRC startup condition.

Statistical summary:

- Parts 8–12: Average 3.96 min — beginning of degradation (+13% above target).
- Parts 13–20: Average 4.96 min — significant degradation (+42% above target).

Pattern diagnosis — Progressive wear-driven slowdown:
The smooth, monotonic increase (each part taking slightly longer than the last) is the textbook signature of a machine or process degrading progressively. Abrupt events (coolant loss, program change) produce step-changes, not smooth ramps. This is a gradual phenomenon.

Most probable causes:

1. Tool wear progression: As the tool wears, the machine's adaptive control (if active) reduces feeds automatically. Even without adaptive control, a worn tool requires higher spindle load, which may trigger speed/feed reductions via safety overrides.

2. Chip packing in a pocket or bore: If chips are not being fully evacuated, the tool re-cuts them on subsequent passes, progressively slowing effective material removal.

3. Thermal growth: As the machine runs, thermal expansion of the spindle and axes can shift critical dimensions, causing the machine to make correction moves or trigger probe re-measurement loops.

Recommended actions:
- Replace the cutting tool immediately and monitor whether the next run restores 3.5-minute cycles.
- Review coolant pressure and flow to the cut zone for chip evacuation.
- Establish a tool change interval at or before part 7 (where degradation begins) for future runs.

Explain what a subroutine is in Fanuc G-code and show an example using M98 and M99.

A subroutine (or subprogram) is a separate, reusable block of G-code that is called from the main program when needed. Subroutines are used to avoid repeating the same code sequences, to make programs shorter and easier to maintain, and to create modular structures for patterns, features, or tool paths that repeat.

M98 — Call subprogram. M98 P__ directs the control to jump to the subprogram with the number matching the P value and begin executing it.
M99 — Return from subprogram. At the end of a subprogram, M99 sends control back to the line immediately after the M98 call in the main program.

Example — Main program calls a subprogram to drill 3 holes at different positions:

Main program (O1000):
O1000
G21 G90 G17 G40 G49 G80
G54
T02 M06
G43 H02 Z50.0
M03 S1800
M08
G00 X20.0 Y20.0
M98 P2000
G00 X50.0 Y20.0
M98 P2000
G00 X80.0 Y20.0
M98 P2000
M09
M05
G00 Z50.0
M30

Subprogram (O2000):
O2000
G81 Z-15.0 R2.0 F120
G80
M99

The main program positions the tool at each XY location, then calls O2000. O2000 executes the drilling cycle at that position, cancels it with G80, and returns via M99.

Repeating a subprogram multiple times:
M98 P2000 L5 — Calls subprogram O2000 five times in succession. Useful for depth pass loops in G91 incremental mode.

Subprograms must be stored in the machine's memory under the O number referenced by M98 P__. They can also be nested (a subprogram calling another subprogram) up to the control's nesting limit, typically 4 levels on Fanuc controls.

What is the difference between climb milling and conventional milling, and how does it affect the G-code direction?

Climb milling and conventional milling describe the relationship between the direction of tool rotation and the direction of feed motion along a profile. This does not change which G-code commands you use, but it determines which direction (G41/left or G42/right compensation, and CW/CCW path direction) you program.

Conventional milling (up milling):
The tool rotates against the direction of feed. The chip starts at zero thickness and grows to maximum at exit. The cutting forces tend to push the workpiece away from the cutter. Conventional milling is better for worn machines with backlash, for rough cuts on hard or abrasive materials, and for interrupted cuts. The tool tends to rub slightly at entry, generating more heat.

Climb milling (down milling):
The tool rotates in the same direction as the feed. The chip starts at maximum thickness and exits thin. Cutting forces pull the workpiece into the cutter, requiring good fixturing. Climb milling produces better surface finish, longer tool life, and lower cutting temperatures. It is the standard finishing approach on modern, rigid machining centers without backlash.

G-code direction effect:
For an outside rectangular profile viewed from above, milled with a right-hand end mill (M03 clockwise spindle):
- Climb milling (preferred finish): Feed in a clockwise direction around the outside of the part. Use G42 (right compensation) as the tool is to the right of the programmed part edge.
- Conventional milling: Feed counter-clockwise around the outside. Use G41 (left compensation).

For inside pockets:
- Climb milling (finishing pass): Feed counter-clockwise. Use G41.
- Conventional: Feed clockwise. Use G42.

Always default to climb milling on finish passes on rigid machining centers for best surface finish and tool life.

Critical Issue — G12 is not a valid standard Fanuc milling command:
On standard Fanuc 0i/31i controls, G12 is not defined as a G-code and will generate an alarm (Illegal G-code or undefined G-code error) when the control attempts to execute it. The program will not run.

G12 appears in some CAM system post-processors as a shorthand for circular pocket milling (a full circle cut using I as the radius), but this is a machine-builder-specific custom cycle, not a standard Fanuc ISO code. It exists on certain Mazak, Haas (older), and custom-configured machines but is not universally available.

What is likely intended:
If the goal is to mill a circular pocket or circular profile with a 25 mm radius, it should be programmed using standard G02/G03 arcs. For a full 360° circle centered at X50 Y50 with radius 25 mm:

What is feed per tooth, how do I calculate it, and how does it relate to the F word in G-code?

Feed per tooth (FPT), also called chip load, is the thickness of material each cutting edge removes per revolution. It is the fundamental parameter that determines cutting force, chip formation, and tool life. The F word in G-code is the table feed rate in mm/min (or in/min), which is derived from FPT.

Relationship:
Feed rate (mm/min) = FPT × Number of teeth × RPM

Example:
End mill: 4 flutes, 10 mm diameter
Material: Aluminum
Recommended FPT for this tool/material: 0.05 mm/tooth
Spindle speed: S3000 RPM

Feed rate = 0.05 × 4 × 3000 = 600 mm/min → Program F600

Where to get FPT values:
- Tool manufacturer catalogs and speed/feed charts (primary source)
- Machining handbooks (Machinery's Handbook)
- CAM software material libraries

Typical FPT ranges for carbide end mills:
- Aluminum: 0.03–0.10 mm/tooth
- Mild steel: 0.02–0.05 mm/tooth
- Stainless steel: 0.01–0.03 mm/tooth
- Hardened steel: 0.005–0.015 mm/tooth

Why it matters for G-code:
If you program a feed rate that is too low, you are not chip-loading the tool enough — the edge rubs instead of cuts, generating heat and causing premature wear. Too high, and you overload the tool and risk breakage. The F word should always be calculated from FPT, RPM, and flute count — never estimated arbitrarily.

Our machine's positioning accuracy data shows X-axis error increasing: Week 1: ±0.005 mm, Week 2: ±0.008 mm, Week 3: ±0.012 mm, Week 4: ±0.019 mm. Is this a concern?

Yes, this is a significant concern that warrants immediate investigation. The data shows a near-doubling of positioning error every two weeks — this is not normal process variation, it is a progressive mechanical degradation signature.

Error growth analysis:
- Week 1 to Week 4: error grew from ±0.005 to ±0.019 mm — a 280% increase in four weeks.
- The growth rate is accelerating (differences: +0.003, +0.004, +0.007), suggesting the underlying cause is worsening, not stabilizing.
- At this rate, ±0.030 mm or greater could be reached within 2 more weeks.

Most likely causes:

1. Ball screw wear (most probable): Gradual wear of the X-axis ball screw nut increases backlash and reduces repeatability. Modern controls compensate for backlash via parameter settings, but those must be periodically updated as wear progresses.

2. Lubrication starvation: Insufficient lubrication of the ball screw and linear guides accelerates wear and increases positioning error. Verify the automatic lube system is functioning and delivering oil to the X-axis components.

3. Thermal effects: If the machine does not have thermal compensation and is running in a variable-temperature environment, thermal expansion of the ball screw can cause systematic position shift. Measure error at different times of day to isolate this.

4. Encoder or feedback cable degradation: A deteriorating linear scale or encoder can produce increasing position noise.

Immediate actions:
- Perform a ballbar test to quantify the error type (backlash, reversal spike, squareness).
- Check and manually cycle the lubrication system.
- Pull and review the X-axis backlash compensation parameter (Fanuc parameter 1851).
- Contact a service engineer if error continues to grow — ball screw replacement may be needed within the next 1–2 months.

Explain the difference between G98 and G99 in canned cycles.

G98 and G99 control where the tool retracts to after each cycle operation (drill, tap, bore, etc.) in a Fanuc canned cycle.

G99 — Retract to R-plane (default on most controls). After completing the canned cycle operation (drilling to Z depth, for example), the tool retracts back to the R-plane — the clearance plane just above the hole entrance that you defined in the R word. G99 is faster because the Z axis only travels the short distance from Z depth to R. Use G99 when all holes are on the same flat surface with no obstructions between positions.

G98 — Retract to Initial Z plane. After completing the operation, the tool retracts all the way back to the Z height the tool was at before the canned cycle started (the height from the last G00 move before the cycle). G98 is slower because the Z axis retracts much further, but it is safer when clamps, bosses, steps, or other obstructions exist between hole positions that the tool would otherwise hit if it only retracted to the R-plane.

Example comparison:
Assume tool starts at Z50.0, R=2.0, Z depth=-20.0.

G99: After drilling, tool goes to Z2.0 (R-plane). Next XY rapid is at Z2.0.
G98: After drilling, tool goes to Z50.0 (initial Z). Next XY rapid is at Z50.0.

G98 Z travel per hole: 20 + 48 = 68 mm retract. G99 Z travel per hole: 20 + 2 = 22 mm retract.

Best practice: Use G99 for flat parts with no obstacles between holes. Switch to G98 when drilling across stepped surfaces, over clamps, or between raised features where the short R-plane retract would cause a collision.

What is the purpose of the R-plane in drilling canned cycles?

The R-plane (also called the reference plane or retract plane) is a Z-height set just above the workpiece surface in a drilling canned cycle. It serves as the transition point between rapid approach and controlled feed motion.

How it works in a G81 (or G83, G84, etc.) cycle:
1. The tool rapids from the initial Z position down to the R-plane at machine rapid speed.
2. At the R-plane, the motion transitions to the programmed feed rate (F word).
3. The tool feeds at the controlled rate from R down to the Z depth.
4. After completing the operation, the tool retracts (to R in G99, or to initial Z in G98).

Typical R-plane values:
- Flat surface, no obstructions: R2.0 (2 mm above the surface) is standard. This minimizes the fed (non-cutting) approach distance.
- Surfaces with coolant holes, casting flash, or irregular top surfaces: R5.0–R10.0 to ensure the drill is under feed control before it contacts material.
- Through-hole drilling where chips may build up on the exit side: set R on the approach side close to the surface.

Why R-plane location matters:
- Too high: The drill feeds through air for an unnecessarily long distance, wasting cycle time.
- Too low: The drill is still at rapid speed when it contacts the material surface, causing drill tip damage, poor hole quality, or breakage on hard materials.

Example:
G81 X20.0 Y20.0 Z-15.0 R2.0 F120
The drill rapids from Z50 (initial position) to Z2.0 (R-plane), then feeds from Z2.0 to Z-15.0 at F120 mm/min, then rapids back to Z50 (G98) or Z2.0 (G99).

Our scrap rate data: January 0.8%, February 1.1%, March 1.9%, April 3.4%. All the same program, same material vendor. What should we investigate?

A scrap rate that has more than quadrupled in four months with no program or material vendor changes is a serious quality trend. The doubling pattern each month (roughly 0.8 → 1.1 → 1.9 → 3.4) suggests a progressive, compounding degradation rather than a random process shift.

Priority investigation areas:

1. Tooling — most likely root cause:
Tool condition is the most common driver of progressive quality degradation. If tool change intervals were not adjusted as volumes or materials varied, tools may be running well past their optimal life. Pull the last 30 tool changes and check if average tool life (parts per edge) has decreased.

2. Fixturing and workholding wear:
Over thousands of cycles, vise jaws wear, locating pins wear, and clamp repeatability decreases. Measure part-to-fixture repeatability by indicating a reference surface across 10 consecutive setups.

3. Machine calibration drift:
Geometric accuracy (squareness, straightness) drifts over time. A ballbar test and laser calibration check will quantify X/Y/Z positioning error. Cross-reference your scrap failure modes — if parts are failing on dimensional tolerances in one axis, that axis may be drifting.

4. Process parameter drift:
Check if anyone has been adjusting spindle speed overrides, feed overrides, or tool offset values to compensate for perceived issues. These adjustments often mask root causes and introduce new errors.

5. Measurement system variation:
Verify that gauges and measuring equipment are within calibration. A drifting gauge reads more parts as out-of-spec even if the process is unchanged.

Recommended approach: Perform a gauge R&R study, inspect tooling and fixtures, run a ballbar test, and review any operator log notes or informal adjustments made since January.

What is G52 and how does it differ from G54?

G52 is a Local Coordinate System shift command. It temporarily offsets the current active work coordinate system (such as G54) by a specified amount without changing the actual stored G54 values.

Syntax:
G52 X__ Y__ Z__ — Shifts the local origin by the specified amounts.
G52 X0 Y0 Z0 — Cancels the local shift and returns to the original work coordinate system.

How G52 differs from G54:
G54 is a stored machine offset that defines the work zero point. It is set by the operator using the MDI or offset screen and persists between programs and power cycles.

G52 is a temporary programmatic shift applied on top of the active work offset. G52 does not change the stored G54 value — it adds a local shift that only exists while it is active in the current program.

Practical use cases:
1. Patterns with repeated features at different locations: Set G54 at the part origin, then use G52 to shift locally to each feature's sub-origin, run the feature subroutine, and cancel G52 before moving to the next feature.
2. Mirror or offset fixtures: If two identical parts are fixtured at different locations and you want to run the same subprogram for each without changing G54.

Example:
G54 (part origin at X0 Y0)
G52 X50.0 Y30.0 (all moves now reference a local origin 50,30 from G54)
M98 P5000 (run pocket routine referenced to local zero)
G52 X0 Y0 (cancel local shift)

Important: G52 behavior varies slightly between Fanuc control versions. On some controls, G52 is reset by certain G-codes or by a power cycle. Always cancel G52 explicitly with G52 X0 Y0 before program end to avoid leaving a residual shift active for the next program.

What does M19 do in a Fanuc CNC program?

M19 commands spindle orientation — it stops the spindle and rotates it to a specific, repeatable angular position (typically 0°, or a position defined by a machine parameter). The spindle is then held at that angle by the servo system.

Why spindle orientation is needed:

1. Tool changes: The ATC arm must engage the tool holder at an exact angular position to correctly grip and release tools. On most machines, M06 triggers spindle orientation automatically as part of its internal sequence, so programmers rarely need to call M19 explicitly for tool changes.

2. Boring bar orientation: Fine boring bars have a specific cutting tip direction. After boring to size, the bar must be oriented to a precise angle before the tool is retracted radially (using G76 fine boring cycle), otherwise the tip drags across the bored surface on retract, damaging the finish.

3. Broaching or specialized operations: Any operation requiring the spindle to be at a fixed angle for tool insertion or special fixtures.

Syntax:
M19 — Orient to the default position (parameter-defined, typically 0°).
M19 S__ — Orient to a specific angle in degrees (e.g., M19 S90 for 90°), on controls that support angle specification.

After M19, the spindle is locked. To resume normal rotation: program M03 or M04. The spindle will release from orientation and accelerate to the commanded S speed.

Note: M19 is typically called behind the scenes by M06. Explicitly programming M19 is primarily needed for fine boring cycles (G76) or specialized fixturing operations. Do not confuse M19 with M05 — M19 does stop spindle rotation but actively holds the spindle at a fixed angle, whereas M05 simply stops and releases.

Our machine logged these tool breakage events: Tool 3 broke at part #12, #31, #55, #78. The expected life is 100 parts. What does this pattern tell us?

This tool breakage pattern is highly informative. Here is what the data tells us:

Breakage interval analysis:
- Break 1: Part 12 (12 parts into the edge life)
- Break 2: Part 31 (19 parts after the previous break)
- Break 3: Part 55 (24 parts after the previous break)
- Break 4: Part 78 (23 parts after the previous break)

Pattern diagnosis — early and repeated failure, well short of target life:
The tool is consistently breaking at 12–24 parts against a 100-part expected life. This means the tool is failing at 12–24% of its expected life. This is not a random breakage pattern — it is a systematic problem indicating the tool is being consistently overloaded or misapplied.

The increasing interval between breaks (12 → 19 → 24 → 23) could reflect that the operator is noticing the issue and making adjustments (reducing feeds, checking setup) between breaks — or simply natural variation in tool quality within the batch.

Root cause candidates:

1. Incorrect cutting parameters: Feeds and speeds may be too aggressive for the tool grade, workpiece material hardness, or depth of cut. Verify that programmed parameters match the tool manufacturer's recommendations.

2. Excessive radial engagement: If the tool is slotting (100% radial engagement) when it should be doing a trochoidal or partial-engagement path, the chip load is far higher than expected.

3. Poor chip evacuation: Chip packing in pockets or blind features causes re-cutting and dramatically increases instantaneous tool load.

4. Tool runout: Check holder TIR (total indicator reading). Even 0.01–0.02 mm of runout causes one flute to carry a disproportionate chip load, leading to early failure.

5. Wrong tool grade or coating for the material: A general-purpose coating in a material requiring a specialized grade will produce premature failure.

Recommended actions: Measure holder runout, review cutting parameters against the tool datasheet, check chip evacuation, and consider reducing radial engagement using a trochoidal strategy if slotting.

Cancelling G40 on a stationary block at the last programmed point on the part will cause the control to create an incorrect exit move or alarm.

Explain what G17, G18, and G19 are and when each is used.

G17, G18, and G19 select the active machining plane. The selected plane controls three things: (1) the plane in which circular interpolation (G02/G03) is calculated, (2) the plane in which cutter radius compensation (G41/G42) is applied, and (3) the plane referenced by canned cycles.

G17 — XY Plane. The standard plane for vertical milling centers (VMC). Circular arcs and cutter compensation operate in the XY plane. The Z axis is the tool axis (the axis along which the spindle drives into the material). This is the most commonly used plane in milling.

G18 — XZ Plane. Circular arcs and cutter comp operate in the XZ plane. The Y axis is the tool axis. Used when machining on a horizontal spindle machine (HMC) where the spindle is parallel to the X axis, or when programming a tilted rotary axis operation.

G19 — YZ Plane. Circular arcs and cutter comp operate in the YZ plane. The X axis is the tool axis. Used on certain horizontal machining centers and for specific 4-axis or 5-axis operations.

Practical example:
For a VMC cutting a circular pocket in the XY plane (top of the part):

For an HMC with the spindle facing in the Z direction boring a hole in the XY face:
G17 is still used if XY is the cutting plane.

For an HMC where the spindle faces in the Y direction:
G18 or G19 would be used depending on orientation.

In over 95% of VMC milling programs, G17 is the only plane code needed. Always include G17 in your safe startup block to ensure a known plane is selected regardless of what the previous program left active.

This program is well-structured overall but contains one redundant move and one minor observation:

Issue 1 — Redundant first G01 move:
After G01 Z-3.0 F100 (plunge to depth), the next line is G01 X10.0 Y10.0 F250. The tool is already at X10.0 Y10.0 from the G00 positioning block. This G01 move goes to the same position the tool is already at — it is a zero-distance move. While not dangerous, it wastes a line, resets the feed rate to F250 (which is fine), and may cause a brief dwell on some controls. It should be removed.

Corrected sequence after plunge:
G01 Z-3.0 F100
G01 X60.0 Y10.0 F250 (begin cutting immediately)

Issue 2 — Profile does not re-approach start point after plunge:
The current code plunges at the start corner (X10 Y10), which means the tool enters the material at the corner of the profile. Depending on tool type and material, corner entry can create a small witness mark or slight gouging at the entry point. A better practice is to plunge slightly inside or outside the profile and approach the corner with a lead-in arc or line.

Everything else is correctly written: safe startup block, work offset, tool change, TLC, coolant, closed rectangular profile (X10→X60→X60Y40→X10Y40→X10Y10), spindle and coolant off before retract, and M30 ending. This is a well-structured program aside from the redundant move.

Write a complete Fanuc G-code program for a simple contour — milling a full 360° circular boss (outside of a 40mm diameter circle, 3mm depth) centered at X0 Y0, leaving a finished boss.

This program mills the outside of a 40 mm diameter circular boss (20 mm radius) using a climb milling direction with G42 (right cutter compensation), assuming a 10 mm diameter end mill (5 mm radius stored in D01).

O1080
(CIRCULAR BOSS - 40MM DIA, 3MM DEPTH)
(TOOL: T01 - 10MM END MILL, 4 FLUTE)
(G54 ORIGIN: CENTER OF BOSS)
G21 G90 G17 G40 G49 G80
G54
T01 M06
G43 H01 Z50.0
M03 S3820
M08
(APPROACH OUTSIDE PROFILE - LEAD IN FROM +X SIDE)
G00 X35.0 Y0.0
G01 Z-3.0 F120
(ACTIVATE CUTTER COMP RIGHT - CLIMB MILL CW)
G42 D01 G01 X20.0 Y0.0 F380
(FULL CLOCKWISE CIRCLE - START AND END AT X20 Y0)
G02 X20.0 Y0.0 I-20.0 J0.0 F380
(LEAD OUT)
G40 G01 X35.0 Y0.0
M05
M09
G00 Z50.0
M30

Line-by-line explanation:
S3820: Calculated for a 10 mm carbide end mill in aluminum at ~120 SFM×1000/(π×10) = ~3820 RPM. Adjust for your material.
F380: Feed rate = FPT × flutes × RPM = 0.025 × 4 × 3820 ≈ 382 mm/min.
G00 X35 Y0: Approach outside the circle (35 > 20 radius + tool radius of 5 = 25, so we are safely outside).
G42 D01 G01 X20 Y0: Lead-in move activates right compensation, positioning the tool edge on the 20 mm radius. D01 must contain 5.0 (tool radius).
G02 I-20 J0: Full 360° CW circle. End point = start point (X20 Y0). I-20 J0 = center offset from start point (center is at X0 Y0, which is -20 from X20).
G40 G01 X35 Y0: Lead-out move cancels compensation in air.

Verify D01 contains the exact tool radius before running. A wrong offset value in cutter comp will produce an incorrectly sized boss.
